item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, when I got there I thought it was blank on purpose to signify that the universe got blanked. Would've been a bit cheesy, yea, but I liked the brief moment I thought that's what it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that makes sense! And as for the thickness, maybe porthole windows are thicker than usual?

Although the definite answer is up to the author, thank you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The wording is a little bit funky there…. Readers paying less attention to wording would probably interpret it in reverse thinking the glass was cut off somehow by the anomaly’s dimensions… but then again what interior windows need to be over 5 centimeters thick in normal circumstances?

assuming that the way its worded currently is 100% what was intended and not the reverse that one might assume in passing… it could be considered that the porthole glass ’elongates’ when the door is closed such as to cover some distance of the rooms spatial anomaly into the other side? like the pane of glass is traversing the entire span of the closed-door space of the room all the way to the opposite wall in the space of those extra 5 centimeters. When the door is open and its just a window into the room, the pane of glass is a normal thickness. Then, when the door is closed and it becomes a window that exits the opposite wall, it has to stretch and traverse the crumpled pocket space in order to “reach” its exit side.

dunno if that makes sense and I’m sure the author has better insight, but I hope that my interpretation of spacial folding helps

+1 all the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was expecting to see a tragical ending, especially when I saw the last page at the first glance. But this ending is actually much better, despite that I would want a bigger ending scene after five-page increasing tension. I do like optimism nowadays. +1

However I have a question.

Refraction shows that the glass as five centimeters shallower than when the door is closed, suggesting that part of the window is affected by the anomaly.

If the glass is affected, shouldn't it become shallower when the door is closed? But the sentence seems to mean that, when the door is open, the glass becomes shallower than it is when the door is closed. Am I somehow misunderstanding it?

I'm trying to translate this scip into CN, so I'm really looking forward to your response. Thx!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP, couldn't help but notice a typo on the last page, though: you've got a missplaced apostrophe here: "and member's of the Serpent's".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish I could +1 this so many times. I've been lurking on this site for the past decade, and I barely ever comment. I think the reason I loved this scip so much was that it reminds me of the first 2000 in a way I'm not sure I can actually put into words.

The formatting is minimalist and is only there to enhance the piece, not just be shiny eye-candy. It feels so mundane but world-ending at the same time. I love the way humans are directly responsible for this, and that the whole scip is a warning about the hubris of the foundation without being LOLfoundation. I love the way you can interpret the ending as either contained and blacked out due to being O5'd or that reality experienced an XK scenario, and either makes total sense.

Basically, +1 from an old lurker who smiled at a scip that triggered his nostalgia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had some good fun with the rising tension between offsets, and the whole article is a good read. The ending was maybe missing some sting after this anomaly's gradual rise in danger, but maybe that's just me wanting to see the world burn. Seeing as how it's your article and not mine, It's just fine as is. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you did there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a huge fan of the ending tbh (though I admit it is more clever and appropriate here than many similar endings) but the rest of the article is fantastic. + 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. Easy +1 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"we don't demote researcher to D-Class anymore"

there's a very simple solution to that: kick them out of the foundation, THEN recruit them as D-Class for their crimes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this. Do think they probably should have just bricked over the door to the storage room at the start haha. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decent, but imo not grand enough for my idea of an X000. I'll upvote after the contest is done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me likey. Quite mysterious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this piece, very well written, and the mystery was absolutely great, getting to the bottom of it with that final page was satisfying as fuck. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoyed this! Simple, no shock factor, with great SCP Foundation methodical approaches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like adirtyshame, I kind of liked the black screen denoting the XK happened. (Also, wtf is scp-foundation dot com? :O)

Gotta say, I really got into this. Every time I saw another iteration at the bottom of the page, it was a sense of "Holy shit, how much worse can this get?" I love how drilling a hole through the wall let the emptiness leak out. (And how the window didn't; I assume it's because they installed it while the anomaly wasn't active.) I also love that line about the guy's husband never forgiving him for forgetting their anniversary. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
when I saw that the link for the final page had an XK in it, I said "oh. ohhhhhhhh." started to grit my teeth. then i clicked on the link and it was just a black screen. and I went, "welp." I'm glad I took a look at the comments and made the effort to press my finger down for a bit, which highlighted a word of the text. it was satisfying, but i almost found the idea of a purely black screen and what that implies MORE compelling. but it seemed like others weren't happy with the lack of a concrete ending. can't please everyone all the time, right? :-)

I appreciate that despite the many offsets, this is a relatively concise entry. a couple have made my eyes cross just from the sheer length, combined on a couple of occasions w content that just plain didn't grab me.

also, a random little thing I found amusing. in, I think, the 5th page, when I read about Verne, I yelled "you idiot!" at my phone. I was tickled by the fact that Matsons first sentence in the notes on that page were "that idiot!" (hopefully this isn't really a spoiler but I can hide it if necessary.)

as others have mentioned, there are a few things on the wiki fairly similar to this, but its still a solid entry. +1

edited bc I'm an idiot who doesn't understand basic formatting. I give up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, one of my favorites of the 5ks.

Copyedit: offset 4 is missing a "to" in the sentence "we can find a way contain and reverse"

I agree with others that the "blue highlights for new material" format styling would make this much easier and more enjoyable to read. E.g. http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2998

I'm not a fan of the final page's formatting. I suggest rethinking that entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
offset 3 is missing a word

Dr. Verne was among those injured by the incident, so has been removed from the project.

missing a "he" between so and been

+1 tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
mostly just going to second J. Conwell's comments, however, I do think one of those blue "change highlight" formatting tricks would do some good.

all in all a great article though, easy +1

Also in a serious rarity for mainline articles, Dr. Matson is actually a character I'd like to see a bit more of. I'm interested in his new role at the Mnestics Division.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved the slowly increasing tension as I read. I'll have to look at this one on the desktop as well; I usually read on mobile, so I want to see how the final page looks/is acccessible in another format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think if you look closely, you'll be able to put your finger on why that page is there. It's really the highlight of the whole article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The XK page is meant to just be entirely black I hope, or something is broken for me.
Even if it's not, I honestly feel it's unnecessary, there's enough incipient "oh shit" in the Keter iteration without a gimmick final page. Null vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally like this version a lot better. Great work, and I really enjoyed the read!

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded: Fixed the formatting issues. I've also added a bit to the final page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, check it again. And look… look closer if you have to.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

Complained Lodged by Dr. Verne:

Should be a complaint, shouldn't it?

> SCP-5000 is a spatial anomaly manifesting in WIng E of Site 98

In SCP-5000-4-E, the I in wing is incorrectly capitalised.

I'd say the most apparent formatting quirk that I'm not a fan of is the plaintext in the research notes. I'd recommend putting them in quoteblocks/similar, so they don't blend with the actual document as much as they are currently.

All in all, I enjoyed this a reasonable amount. The escalation was well-paced, and the actual anomaly was pretty interesting. I feel the ending may have been obvious a little too soon though — I was fairly sure of what exactly was going to happen as soon as it spread to the hallway for the first time and wasn't surprised by anything for that reason. Despite this, the little differences between the offsets and the researcher's notes were compelling enough for me to upvote. And while this form of ending has been done to some extent in the past, it was sufficiently unique in other aspects that that didn't concern me.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have an idea that might make things more satisfying for you. I'll just have to see if I can develop the skill to pull it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it is better now, but I agree with what ModernMajorGeneral said about it being the weakest part of a strong article. I think part of the issue for me is that when you read the last page, you see the "XK" document link before you see Dr. Matson's notes, so the last concept I was left with was Matson saying that they could still turn it around.

Still a +1, but I feel like the ending is unsatisfying to me compared to an interesting setup and premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an improvement. The link to the site was confusing but also made me think that they opened a hole into our reality or something and turned it into a meta scp (are there still undertones of this?)

I still think that the ending is the weakest part of this - 'yeah reality disappeared, the end' was underwhelming - but it was the weakest part of a strong article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Actually, I've just changed the link a bit. Try it now and tell me if that seems clear enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the idea is, with the designation of the page, that things have not gone well. The redirect to the main page was just meant to mean "You're done here, move along." That seems to be confusing people (and not just the two of you; I've had complaints elsewhere), so I've removed the link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I was wondering about this. I was enjoying this until that happened, with my initial reaction being 'oh no, the last page hasn't been uploaded' followed by 'oh no, it was another one of those SCPs all along'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had the same question, am I missing something? I don't really understand the point of it, it would be great if someone could explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the last link supposed to go to the main page?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmmm. Damn I love the concept here.

I read a few preliminary versions of this a while back, and I'm still enamoured with it today.

You've still got it, Mann. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made some minor edits to version 5-K to clarify things a bit, and also fixed the "spacial" instead of "spatial" error throughout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is every bit as incredible as it was when I first read it months ago. There are formatting things that could be improved, but conceptually and narratively this article strikes a chord with me that not many others in the contest have thus far.

Good job, Mann.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to see this got posted.

All in all, I really like this. As someone who is warry of the "rinse and repeat" style of offsets, the changes between iterations are drastic enough that treading over the old ground is not dull. Additionally, the mystery of what the room's deal is that gets slowly unraveled across the various iterations I found to be quite compelling.

If I did have one complaint, its that we have seen the "We've done this before" story thread a few times on the SCP wiki already (most notably in SCP-2000), though your take here isn't bad by any stretch.

+1 Mann. A good addition to the contest.

item.title: The Empty Rooms
item.content:encoded:
There we go, just under the wire! SCP-5000, The Empty Rooms! Please enjoy this not-so-locked room mystery.

EDIT: My slot requests are as followed:

5999, 5001, 5423, 5099, and 5500.
